Curves in the complex plane

Suppose the continuous real-valued functions @@math:8:14bd01@@ @@math:9:367f4e@@ @@math:10:c2a5a2@@ are parametric equations of a curve @@math:11:ff2553@@ in the complex plane. If we use these equations as the real and imaginary parts in @@math:12:5e0a45@@ we can describe the points @@math:13:dabed1@@ on @@math:14:f81cb8@@ by means of a complex-valued function of a real variable @@math:15:ee884d@@ called a parametrization of @@math:16:d35f4b@@: @@math:6:3639f7@@

The point @@math:17:cc3c71@@ or @@math:18:04ed65@@ is called the initial point of @@math:19:3f39a4@@ and @@math:20:5f7a70@@ or @@math:21:6eff1b@@ is its terminal point. The expression @@math:22:4593a6@@ could also be interpreted as a two-dimensional vector function. Consequently, @@math:23:65450c@@ and @@math:24:de316b@@ can be interpreted as position vectors. As @@math:25:e7b47b@@ varies from @@math:26:151cb8@@ to @@math:27:1e512b@@ we can envision the curve @@math:28:ad8559@@ being traced out by the moving arrowhead of @@math:29:bc2d44@@ This can be appreciated in the following applet with @@math:30:7c19bb@@

Press Start to animate. You can move the points to change the curve.

For example, the parametric equations @@math:31:45ae6e@@ @@math:32:f0a244@@ @@math:33:965c6b@@ describe a unit circle centered at the origin. A parametrization of this circle is @@math:34:180b71@@ or @@math:35:8e163a@@ @@math:36:a408ec@@

Contours

The notions of curves in the complex plane that are smooth, piecewise smooth, simple, closed, and simple closed are easily formulated in terms of the vector function (\ref{parcurve}). Suppose the derivative of (\ref{parcurve}) is @@math:37:16a81b@@ We say a curve @@math:38:f7b017@@ in the complex plane is smooth if @@math:39:2098d6@@ is continuous and never zero in the interval @@math:40:6f189b@@ As shown in Figure 1, since the vector @@math:41:cce075@@ is not zero at any point @@math:42:447e9b@@ on @@math:43:02bf12@@ the vector @@math:44:67a243@@ is tangent to @@math:45:0bb6ab@@ at @@math:46:ce9016@@ Thus, a smooth curve has a continuously turning tangent; or in other words, a smooth curve can have no sharp corners or cusps. See Figure 2 for a counterexample.

A piecewise smooth curve @@math:49:83f885@@ has a continuously turning tangent, except possibly at the points where the component smooth curves @@math:50:93d490@@ are joined together.

A curve @@math:51:8d38b4@@ in the complex plane is said to be a simple if @@math:52:ef312f@@ for @@math:53:a70470@@ except possibly for @@math:54:892114@@ and @@math:55:3cebc0@@ @@math:56:d42b9c@@ is a closed curve if $z(a) = z(b).$

@@math:57:816c5f@@ is a simple closed curve if @@math:58:8419e1@@ for @@math:59:1bface@@ and @@math:60:0c9161@@

In complex analysis, a piecewise smooth curve @@math:61:e4575c@@ is called a contour or path. We define the positive direction on a contour @@math:62:a24581@@ to be the direction on the curve corresponding to increasing values of the parameter @@math:63:20787d@@ It is also said that the curve @@math:64:03dd24@@ has positive orientation. In the case of a simple closed contour @@math:65:7076ca@@ the positive direction corresponds to the counterclockwise direction. For example, the circle @@math:66:a38dd5@@ @@math:67:ea8206@@ has positive orientation. The negative direction on a contour @@math:68:7283d9@@ is the direction opposite the positive direction. If @@math:69:b4ad31@@ has an orientation, the opposite curve, that is, a curve with opposite orientation, is denoted by @@math:70:2fa3b7@@ On a simple closed curve, the negative direction corresponds to the clockwise direction. For instance, the circle @@math:71:f475b2@@ @@math:72:4a4a24@@ has negative orientation.
